elements.sent-2411-001: In this article we classify totally geodesic submanifolds of homogeneous nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds, and of the $\mathsf{G _2$-cones over these $6$-manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-002: To this end, we develop new techniques for the study of totally geodesic submanifolds of analytic Riemannian manifolds, naturally reductive homogeneous spaces and Riemannian cones.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, we obtain an example of a totally geodesic submanifold with self-intersections in a simply connected homogeneous space.
elements.sent-2411-004: An almost Hermitian structure on a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is a $(1,1)$ tensor field $J$ preserving the Riemannian metric $g$ and satisfying the identity $J^2=-\operatorname{Id}$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Gray and Hervella~\cite{grayhervella showed that there are 16 natural classes of almost Hermitian structures.}
elements.sent-2411-006: A nice example of those are Kähler structures, which are characterized by the equation $\nabla J=0$, where $\nabla$ denotes the Levi-Civita connection of $(M,g)$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Among the non-integrable Hermitian structures, the nearly Kähler structures are particularly noteworthy.
elements.sent-2411-008: An almost Hermitian structure $J$ on a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is nearly K\"ahler if it satisfies \begin{equation}(\nabla_X J)(Y) =-(\nabla_Y J)(X) \quad for all vector fields $X$ and $Y$ of $M$.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}(\nabla_X J)(Y) =-(\nabla_Y J)(X) \quad for all vector fields $X$ and $Y$ of $M$.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-009: The study of nearly Kähler geometry is particularly interesting in dimension $6$, as strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds (that is, those which are not Kähler) are automatically Einstein, and their Riemannian cones are 7-manifolds with holonomy groups contained in $\s{G _2$, see~\cite{bar .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Indeed, in~\cite{bryant , Bryant constructed the first examples of manifolds with holonomy exactly equal to $\s{G _2$, one of which is a Riemannian cone over the flag manifold $\s{F (\C^3)$ equipped with a nearly Kähler metric.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Although investigations about nearly Kähler manifolds began in the 1950s, significant progress has been made in the recent decades.
elements.sent-2411-012: In 2005, Butruille~\cite{Butruille classified simply connected, homogeneous strictly nearly Kähler manifolds of dimension six.}
elements.sent-2411-013: These are: \begin{equation} S^6 =\s{G}_2/\s{SU}(3), \qquad \C\s{P}^3 =\s{Sp}(2)/(\s{U}(1)\times\s{Sp}(1)), F(\C^3) =\s{SU}(3)/\s{T}^2, \qquad \s{S}^3\times\s{S}^3 =\s{SU}(2)^3/\Delta \s{SU}(2). \end{equation} More recently, in 2017, Foscolo and Haskins~\cite{foscolo produced the first inhomogeneous nearly Kähler structures on $\s{S ^6$ and $\s{S ^3\times\s{S ^3$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These structures are of cohomogeneity one and are $\s{SU (2)\times\s{SU (2)$-invariant.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The aforementioned nearly Kähler manifolds constitute all the known simply connected strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds so far.
elements.sent-2411-016: The main goal of this article is to classify totally geodesic submanifolds in homogeneous strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-017: For this purpose, we develop some general tools for the study of totally geodesic submanifolds of naturally reductive homogeneous spaces.
elements.sent-2411-018: Moreover, we also classify maximal totally geodesic submanifolds of the $\s{G _2$-cones over homogeneous strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The theory of totally geodesic submanifolds in homogeneous spaces is especially fruitful due to the large isometry groups of these spaces.
elements.sent-2411-020: Symmetric spaces are a particular type of homogeneous spaces with an isometric geodesic reflection at each point.
elements.sent-2411-021: Totally geodesic submanifolds of symmetric spaces have been extensively studied, see~e.g.~\cite{BO1,chen1,chen2,exceptional .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the settting of symmetric spaces, totally geodesic submanifolds are orbits of the action of some Lie group acting isometrically, and can be characterized algebraically by means of the so-called Lie triple systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: That being said, the classification of totally geodesic submanifolds in classical symmetric spaces of rank greater than two remains widely open.
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast, in a non-symmetric Riemannian homogeneous space we lack a nice algebraic characterization of totally geodesic submanifolds.
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, although these submanifolds are intrinsically homogeneous, they are not necessarily orbits of Lie groups acting isometrically, see~\cite{nikolayevskytg,RodriguezVazquezOlmos .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Another relevant aspect is that in the (non-symmetric) homogeneous setting, it is natural to consider totally geodesic immersions that are not necessarily injective.
elements.sent-2411-027: The theory of totally geodesic immersions, developed in \cite{Tsukada and \cite{HeintzeLiuOlmos , is central to this work, and in this article we aim to further build upon it, see~Section~\ref{sec:tganalytic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is well known that in homogeneous spaces, every one-dimensional totally geodesic submanifold is injectively immersed (in other words, every geodesic loop is a closed geodesic), and in symmetric spaces, all totally geodesic submanifolds are injectively immersed.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work, we present what is, up to the best of our knowledge, the first example of a totally geodesic submanifold in a simply connected homogeneous space with self-intersections, see~Subsection~\ref{subsec:projplanerealflag .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this article, we introduce the class of $D$-invariant totally geodesic submanifolds of a reductive homogeneous space, where $D$ denotes the difference between the Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$ and the canonical connection $\nabla^c$, see Subsection~\ref{subsec:homspaces .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the setting of naturally reductive homogeneous spaces, which includes symmetric spaces, $D$-invariant totally geodesic submanifolds are orbits of Lie groups acting isometrically, and they admit a nice algebraic description similar to that of Lie triple systems in symmetric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, totally geodesic submanifolds of symmetric spaces are trivially $D$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, the class of $D$-invariant totally geodesic submanifolds seems to be a natural generalization of totally geodesic submanifolds of symmetric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-034: As we will see, not all totally geodesic submanifolds in a naturally reductive homogeneous space are $D$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-035: It will follow from our classification that all maximal totally geodesic submanifolds, i.e.\ those not contained in any other proper totally geodesic submanifold, in the homogeneous nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds are $D$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-036: As we have seen, there are exactly four examples of simply connected homogeneous nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-037: The sphere $\s{S ^6=\s{G _2/\s{SU (3)$, whose nearly Kähler structure is induced by octonionic multiplication, was the first one that appeared.}}}

elements.sent-2411-042: A twistor fibration of an oriented Riemannian $4$-manifold $N$ is a fiber bundle $\pi\colon M\rightarrow N$, where each fiber over $p\in N$ is equal to the set of complex structures of $T_p N$ which preserve the orientation and the Riemannian metric of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-043: It turns out that $\pi$ is an $\s{S ^2$-bundle over $N$, and the twistor space $M$ admits two different natural almost Hermitian structures.}
elements.sent-2411-044: One of them is called the Atiyah-Hitchin-Singer structure and it is integrable if and only if the $4$-manifold $N$ is self-dual, see~\cite{atiyah .}
elements.sent-2411-045: The other one is the Eells-Salamon structure, which can be obtained from the Atiyah-Hitchin-Singer structure by changing the sign only in the fibers.
elements.sent-2411-046: The nearly Kähler structures of $\C\s{P ^3$ and $\s{F (\C^3)$ that we are considering are precisely the Eells-Salamon structures that we get when $N$ is the round sphere $\s{S ^4$ and the complex projective plane $\C\s{P ^2$, respectively.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-049: Following~\cite{RodriguezVazquezOlmos , we say that a totally geodesic submanifold $\Sigma$ of the total space of a Riemannian submersion $M$ is well-positioned if \begin{equation}T_p \Sigma= (V_p \cap T_p \Sigma) \oplus (H_p \cap T_p \Sigma) \quad for all $p\in\Sigma$,\end{equation} where $\mathcal{V}$ and $\mathcal{H}$ denote the vertical and horizontal distributions associated with the Riemannian submersion $F\rightarrow M\rightarrow B$.}

elements.section-2411-003: Totally geodesic immersions in real analytic Riemannian manifolds
elements.sent-2411-272: In this section we develop some new tools in the theory of totally geodesic immersions in analytic Riemannian manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-273: To conduct the global study of totally geodesic submanifolds, it is natural to assume that the ambient space is real analytic, since homogeneous spaces are examples of real analytic manifolds~\cite[Proposition~4.2]{KobayashiNomizu .}
elements.sent-2411-274: As we will see, it is also necessary to allow self-interesctions in our submanifolds, leading to the study of totally geodesic immersions in analytic Riemannian manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-275: Let $M$ be a Riemannian manifold and $f\colon \Sigma \to M$ an isometric immersion of a connected Riemannian manifold $\Sigma$ to $M$.
elements.sent-2411-276: We say that $f$ is a totally geodesic immersion (and $\Sigma$ is a totally geodesic submanifold of $M$) if for every geodesic $\gamma$ of $\Sigma$, the composition $f\circ \gamma$ is also a geodesic of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-277: This condition is equivalent to the vanishing of the second fundamental form $\mathbb{II}$ of $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-278: We refer the reader to~\cite[Appendix~A]{HeintzeLiuOlmos and~\cite[Section~10.3]{BerndtConsoleOlmos for more details on totally geodesic immersions.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: A similar treatment can be found in~\cite{Tsukada .}
elements.sent-2411-280: When studying general totally geodesic immersions, certain redundancies arise that need to be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-281: The first one is due to reparametrizations of the domain, which is handled by introducing the notion of equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-282: We say that two totally geodesic immersions $f_{i}\colon \Sigma_{i}\to M$ are equivalent if there exists an isometry $h\colon \Sigma_{1}\to \Sigma_{2}$ such that $f_{2 =f_{1}\circ h$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: The second redundancy occurs when considering surjective local isometries.
elements.sent-2411-284: For instance, a totally geodesic embedding of $\R\s{P ^2$ in $\R\s{P ^3$ yields the same information as its composition $\s{S ^{2}\to\R\s{P ^{3}$ with the universal cover $\s{S ^{2}\to\R\s{P ^{2}$, though the latter is not an embedding.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: We can deal with this issue by working exclusively with compatible immersions.
elements.sent-2411-286: Let $f\colon \Sigma^{k}\to M$ be totally geodesic, and consider the Grassmann bundle $\s{G _{k (TM)$ of $k$-planes in $TM$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: Then $f$ induces a smooth map $\tilde{f}\colon \Sigma \to \s{G _{k (TM)$ by letting $\tilde{f (p)=(f(p),f_{*p (T_{p}\Sigma))$ (for ease of notation, we will simply write $\tilde{f (p)=f_{*p (T_{p}\Sigma)$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: The map $f$ is said to be compatible if $\tilde{f}$ is injective (this is the case, for example, if $f$ is injective).
elements.sent-2411-289: In other words, we allow $\Sigma$ to have self-intersections in $M$, but at each point of self-intersection the corresponding tangent spaces must be different.
elements.sent-2411-290: It turns out that any totally geodesic immersion can be factored through a compatible one~\cite[p.~272]{BerndtConsoleOlmos , so imposing this condition does not cause us to lose information.}
elements.sent-2411-291: We note that in general, given compatible totally geodesic immersions $f_{1}\colon \Sigma_{1}\to\Sigma_{2}$ and $f_{2}\colon \Sigma_{2}\to M$, the composition $f_{2}\circ f_{1}$ may not be compatible.
elements.sent-2411-292: A counterexample will be provided in Remark~\ref{remark:CompositionCompatibleImmersions .}
elements.sent-2411-293: One sees that a compatible immersion $f\colon \Sigma \to M$ is determined up to equivalence by the image $\tilde{f (\Sigma)\subseteq \s{G _{k (TM)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-294: Given two compatible totally geodesic immersions $f_{i}\colon \Sigma_{i}\to M$, we say that $f_{2}\colon \Sigma_{2}\to M$ extends $f_{1}\colon \Sigma_{1}\to M$ if there exists an injective local isometry $j\colon \Sigma_{1}\to \Sigma_{2}$ satisfying $f_{1 =f_{2}\circ j$, or equivalently, if $\tilde{f _{1 (\Sigma_{1 )\subseteq \tilde{f _{2 (\Sigma_{2 )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-295: This defines a partial ordering in the set of equivalence classes of compatible totally geodesic immersions.
elements.sent-2411-296: By~\cite[Proposition~10.3.2]{BerndtConsoleOlmos , every compatible totally geodesic immersion can be extended to an inextendable one (that is, an immersion which is maximal with respect to the partial ordering), and this extension is unique up to equivalence (we say in this case that the submanifold is inextendable\footnote{Note that Heintze, Liu and Olmos~\cite{HeintzeLiuOlmos refer to these submanifolds as maximal totally geodesic submanifolds, whereas we prefer to reserve this term for another concept.. ).}}}

elements.subsection-2411-011: Totally geodesic submanifolds of Riemannian cones
elements.sent-2411-722: We now discuss the relationship between the totally geodesic submanifolds of a Riemannian cone (over a complete real analytic manifold) and those of its base.
elements.sent-2411-723: We will be interested in determining the maximal totally geodesic submanifolds of the cone $\hat M$ over $M$.
elements.sent-2411-724: This first result shows that every totally geodesic submanifold of the base induces a totally geodesic submanifold of the cone by means of the cone map.
elements.sent-2411-725: Let $M$ be a Riemannian manifold and $\phi\colon S\to M$ an isometric immersion of a $k$-dimensional submanifold $S$.
elements.sent-2411-726: The immersion $\phi$ is totally geodesic if and only if the cone map $\hat{\phi}\colon \hat{S}\to \hat{M}$ is totally geodesic.
elements.sent-2411-727: The totally geodesic immersion $\phi$ is compatible if and only if the cone map $\hat{\phi}\colon \hat{S}\to \hat{M}$ is compatible.

elements.sent-2411-1181: In 2005, Butruille~\cite{Butruille classified all simply connected, homogeneous, strictly nearly Kähler manifolds of dimension six.}
elements.sent-2411-1182: The aforementioned examples constitute all the known simply connected strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds so far.
elements.sent-2411-1183: In this setting, it turns out that totally geodesic submanifolds arise as orbits of isometric actions and can be characterized algebraically by means of the so-called Lie triple systems.
elements.sent-2411-1184: It will follow from our classification that all maximal totally geodesic submanifolds---that is, those that are not contained in any other proper totally geodesic submanifold---in the homogeneous nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds are $D$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-1185: As we have seen, there are exactly four examples of simply connected homogeneous strictly nearly Kähler $6$-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-1186: The sphere $\s{S ^6=\s{G _2/\s{SU (3)$, whose nearly Kähler structure is induced by octonionic multiplication, was the first one to appear.}}}
